Claude Code Skills

AI
Developer Tools
Claude Code
40+ skills for AI-powered development workflows
Published

March 19, 2026

What Are Skills?

Claude Code skills are reusable instruction sets that extend what Claude can do. Think of them as specialized modes — each one teaches Claude how to approach a specific task. Invoke /skill-name and Claude operates with that set of knowledge, constraints, and steps.

This page distinguishes between skills I built from scratch and tools I use heavily (with credit to their authors — that context matters).

Skills I Built

These are original skills I designed and wrote:

411 Case — Interactive business case analysis agent based on the 411 course methodology (David J. Bryce, BYU Marriott). Guides through a structured consulting engagement: problem framing → solution development → assumption mapping → recommendation.

Email Tracker — Reads AI/Claude Code newsletter emails via Gmail MCP, summarizes findings, researches links, and proposes updates to Claude setup files. Automated research pipeline, not just a reader.

Autoresearch — Self-improving skill optimizer. Runs a skill against test prompts, grades outputs against KPIs (phase-level + holistic), and iteratively improves the skill prompt. Inspired by Andrej Karpathy’s autoresearch methodology — I adapted it specifically for Claude Code skill optimization.

Dung — Parallel worktree orchestrator. Reads a project roadmap, identifies tasks that can run in parallel via dependency resolution, creates git worktrees, and outputs ready-to-run terminal commands. Named for the workhorse it is.

Close Session — End-of-session capture routine. 6 phases: project inventory → decisions made → memory updates → git cleanup → summary. Human-gated at every step. Prevents context loss between conversations.

Locker Router — Thin router that fetches skills from a remote Locker server. One skill that gives Claude access to all team skills stored server-side — the last mile of Locker’s delivery system.

KPI Setter — Maps any multi-step process into phases, defines binary KPIs per phase, and writes test scenarios. Pipeline: /kpi-setter → kpi.md → /autoresearch.

Brain Dump — Processes raw thoughts into organized memory files. For when you have 10 minutes and a lot in your head.

Tools I Use Heavily

Credit where it’s due. These tools shaped how I work day to day.

gstack (by Garry Tan)

A suite of skills for shipping software. I use these constantly:

  • /browse — Persistent headless Chromium, ~100ms per command. Navigate, click, screenshot, fill forms, check console errors.
  • /qa — Find bugs, fix with atomic commits, re-verify with screenshots. Loop until clean.
  • /ship — Fully automated release: merge base branch, run tests, audit diff, bump version, update CHANGELOG, commit, push, create PR.
  • /review — Pre-landing PR review. Pass 1: critical issues. Pass 2: informational. Auto-fixes obvious problems.
  • /plan-ceo-review — Founder mode: challenge the scope, find the 10-star product, expand or cut with conviction.
  • /plan-eng-review — Eng manager mode: lock in architecture, diagrams, edge cases, test coverage.
  • /qa-design-review — Visual audit. Grades A-F. Detects AI slop patterns, spacing issues, hierarchy problems. Auto-fixes iteratively.

gstack is the backbone of how I ship. The /browse + /qa + /ship loop is something I run on every project.

Karpathy’s Autoresearch

Andrej Karpathy published an autoresearch methodology: use AI to iteratively improve its own research and reasoning processes. His framing — run the process, grade the output, feed the grade back in, repeat — became the conceptual foundation for my own autoresearch skill, which applies that loop specifically to Claude Code skill optimization. The /kpi-setter → /autoresearch pipeline is a direct descendant of his work.

Anthropic’s Official Skills

Skills from Anthropic’s skills repository that I’ve installed and use regularly: /pdf, /pptx, /docx, /mcp-builder, /doc-coauthoring, /skill-creator, and others.

By the Numbers

  • 47 skills installed system-wide (available across all projects)
  • Sources: built from scratch, adapted from open source, curated from trusted repos (gstack, Anthropic, Vercel Labs, community)
  • Skills are lazy-loaded — they only consume context when invoked
